summary: Jeff Atwood examines the absurdity of End User License Agreements (EULAs), arguing that no one actually reads them because they're impenetrable legalese that users must accept to use software. He highlights the EFF's findings about problematic EULA clauses that restrict free speech, enable monitoring, forbid reverse-engineering, and waive consumer protections. The post questions whether these click-wrap and shrink-wrap licenses are even legally enforceable, ultimately suggesting that ignoring EULAs entirely may be the most rational response to an irrational system.
key_points: EULAs are essentially unreadable contracts that users blindly accept, potentially agreeing to invasive terms like monitoring or liability waivers
Common EULA problems include bans on public criticism, forced automatic updates that compromise privacy, prohibitions on reverse-engineering, and disclaimers of liability for faulty software
The phrase 'READ CAREFULLY' in caps effectively means 'IGNORE THIS' because the terms are non-negotiable and often unreasonable
Both click-wrap (clicking accept) and shrink-wrap (opening packaging) licenses have questionable legal enforceability
The current EULA system creates an impossible situation where reading every agreement would be impractical, yet not reading them means accepting potentially harmful terms
money_quotes: For all I know, the EULA could specify that the software is going to install a keylogger, steal all my passwords and financial information, send incriminating emails threatening the president, format my hard drive, and then sleep with my wife.
I'm an end user, and I don't recall anything good ever coming from clicking that 'I accept' option.
The phrase means, 'IGNORE THIS.' That's because the small print is unchangeable and outrageous.
Why read the 'agreement' if you know that: No sane person would agree to its text, and Even if you disagree, no one will negotiate a better agreement with you?
Given the insanity of our current predicament, not reading the EULA could very well be the most rational course of action.
It's just another meaningless hoop I have to jump through before I can actually use the software.
key_insight: The EULA system is fundamentally broken because it demands users accept non-negotiable, unreadable contracts, making willful ignorance paradoxically the most rational user behavior.
